frameworks.name: OpenAI Swarm (→ Agents SDK)
frameworks.decomposition: Agents encapsulate instructions + functions. Functions can return another Agent, triggering a handoff. No explicit task decomposition—agents self-decide when to hand off via tool calls. Triage agent pattern routes to specialist agents.
frameworks.communication: Agents share conversation history (messages array) and context_variables dict. Only the active agent's system prompt is present. Handoffs pass the full message history to the next agent.
frameworks.merge: No explicit merge primitive. Final agent's response is the output. Context variables accumulate across handoffs. Results from multiple agents remain in the messages array.
strengths: Extremely lightweight—two primitives: Agent and handoff
Stateless between calls, easy to test
Function-as-handoff is elegant and composable
Context variables provide cross-agent state without coupling
Educational simplicity—easy to reason about
weaknesses: Now deprecated in favor of OpenAI Agents SDK
No built-in parallel execution of agents
No DAG or branching—strictly sequential handoffs
Requires OpenAI API (not applicable to our environment)
No built-in memory or persistence layer
Error recovery is purely LLM-driven

frameworks.name: LangGraph
frameworks.decomposition: Tasks are nodes in a directed graph. Each node is a function or agent step. Edges define control flow (conditional, parallel, or fixed). State object flows through the graph, accumulating results.
frameworks.communication: Shared state object (TypedDict) passes between nodes. Each node reads from and writes to shared state. Conditional edges inspect state to determine next node. Subgraphs enable hierarchical decomposition.
frameworks.merge: State accumulation is the merge mechanism. Each node adds to the shared state. Fan-in nodes collect results from parallel branches. Reducers handle conflicting updates to the same state key.
strengths: Explicit DAG control flow—easy to reason about and debug
Durable execution with checkpoint/resume across failures
Built-in human-in-the-loop via state inspection/interrupts
Short-term + long-term memory (working + persistent)
Parallel branching natively supported
Subgraphs enable hierarchical composition
weaknesses: Heavier dependency footprint (LangChain ecosystem)
Steeper learning curve for state management
Designed for Python with specific SDK—hard to replicate without it
State schema requires typing discipline
Overkill for simple linear workflows

frameworks.name: CrewAI
frameworks.decomposition: Agents have roles, goals, and backstories. Tasks are assigned to specific agents with descriptions and expected outputs. Crews run tasks sequentially or hierarchically (manager agent delegates). Flows provide event-driven orchestration with @start/@listen/@router decorators.
frameworks.communication: Agents communicate through task outputs passed as context. Crews share no direct channel—outputs flow through task dependencies. Flows use structured state (Pydantic models) with event listeners. Logical operators (or_, and_) combine trigger conditions.
frameworks.merge: Crew output is the final task result. Flow state accumulates across listeners. Router decorators branch execution based on state values. Human-in-the-loop at task level for review/input.
strengths: Clean role-based mental model—intuitive for team metaphor
YAML configuration separates agent/task definitions from code
Flows add precise control beyond autonomous crews
Pydantic state models provide type safety
Growing community and ecosystem of examples
Supports local models via Ollama/LMStudio
weaknesses: Python-only, significant dependency chain
Crew autonomy can be unpredictable—hard to debug
YAML configs become unwieldy at scale
No built-in parallel execution within a crew
Telemetry by default (privacy concern)
Over-engineered for small numbers of agents

frameworks.name: AutoGen (→ Microsoft Agent Framework)
frameworks.decomposition: Core API uses message-passing and event-driven agents. AgentChat API provides higher-level patterns: two-agent chat, group chat, and agent-as-tool (one agent delegates to another via tool call). Layered design: Core → AgentChat → Extensions.
frameworks.communication: Agents communicate via messages through a runtime. GroupChat uses a round-robin or speaker-selection strategy. Agent-as-tool wraps a sub-agent as a callable tool the parent agent invokes. MCP server integration for tool connectivity.
frameworks.merge: Group chat manager collects responses. Agent-as-tool returns sub-agent's response as tool output. No built-in state merging—relies on conversation history. Extensions provide code execution and file handling.
strengths: Layered architecture allows multiple abstraction levels
Agent-as-tool is a clean composition pattern
MCP support for external tool integration
Cross-language (.NET and Python)
AutoGen Studio provides no-code GUI prototyping
Magentic-One shows SOTA multi-agent team capability
weaknesses: Now in maintenance mode—no new features
Complex API surface with multiple packages
GroupChat can have coordination overhead
Requires OpenAI/Azure by default for full capability
Migration needed to Microsoft Agent Framework
Documentation fragmentation between versions

frameworks.name: Semantic Kernel (→ Microsoft Agent Framework)
frameworks.decomposition: Kernel acts as central orchestrator. Agents use plugins (native functions, prompt templates, OpenAPI specs, MCP). Process Framework models complex business processes as steps with inputs/outputs. Multi-agent via agent-as-plugin pattern.
frameworks.communication: Kernel passes function outputs between components. Plugin chaining: output of one kernel function feeds into next. Agents can be added as plugins to other agents. Thread-based conversation history for multi-turn chat.
frameworks.merge: Kernel function pipeline handles sequential merging. Process Framework steps have explicit input/output contracts. Structured output via Pydantic schemas. No built-in fan-in/merge for parallel branches.
strengths: Model-agnostic—any LLM provider
Plugin ecosystem is rich and extensible
Process Framework suits enterprise workflows
Multi-language (Python, .NET, Java)
Built-in vector DB integration for RAG
Enterprise-grade observability and security
weaknesses: Heavy framework—significant dependency cost
Complexity is high for simple use cases
Now being folded into Microsoft Agent Framework
.NET-centric design pattern feels foreign in Python
Plugin model adds indirection that can obscure flow
Process Framework is rigid for exploratory AI work

key_insights: ALL five frameworks converge on the same primitive: agents are functions with tools/instructions, and orchestration is about routing data between them. The differences are in HOW routing happens (handoff vs graph vs event).

Three reusable patterns emerge from cross-framework analysis: (1) Triage Router—a single entry agent that delegates to specialists (Swarm, CrewAI, AutoGen), (2) DAG Pipeline—explicit directed graph with shared state (LangGraph, SK Process Framework), (3) Agent-as-Tool—wrap sub-agents as tools the parent can invoke (AutoGen, SK).

State management is the hardest problem in multi-agent systems. LangGraph's shared-state approach is most robust; Swarm's context_variables is simplest but error-prone. Our JSON-based state passing between scripts is closer to the DAG pattern.

No framework supports our zero-external-dependency constraint. All require at minimum an LLM API key. Our OpenCode environment must implement patterns using: (a) task tool for subagents, (b) JSON files for state, (c) PowerShell/Python for coordination logic.
The Agent-as-Tool pattern maps directly to OpenCode's task tool: a parent agent dispatches work to subagents and awaits results. This is already our native pattern and should be the foundation.

Parallel execution is poorly supported by all frameworks at the orchestration level. LangGraph has parallel branches, but most require explicit fan-in logic. Our parallel-worktree skill and concurrent task tool calls already exceed what most frameworks offer for parallel dispatch.

Human-in-the-loop is an afterthought in most frameworks (only LangGraph has it built-in). Our verify subagent and verification-before-completion gate are a stronger pattern than any framework provides natively.

Memory and persistence is the biggest gap across all frameworks. LangGraph has checkpointing; others rely on external stores. Our local memory integration plus xTrace/error-registry provide a more comprehensive memory layer than any single framework.
hybrid_pattern_description: Combine the Triage Router pattern (from Swarm/CrewAI) with DAG-based state passing (from LangGraph), using our existing task tool as the Agent-as-Tool primitive. A triage subagent or routing script parses the input, builds a JSON state object, and dispatches to specialist subagents via task tool calls. Each specialist reads from state, performs work, writes results back to state, and returns. A merge/reduce step collects all results. The coordinator script (PowerShell or Python) maintains the state file and controls the DAG execution, with no external dependencies beyond the LLM accessed through OpenCode's built-in model.
implementation_notes: Phase 1: State-as-JSON—each subagent receives a JSON state file path, reads its input slice, writes its output slice, and exits.
Phase 2: Routing script—PowerShell/Python script parses task intent, constructs initial state, launches subagents in sequence or parallel per DAG definition.
Phase 3: DAG definition file—JSON config with nodes (subagent specs), edges (dependencies), and merge rules. Coordinator reads DAG, executes in topological order.
Phase 4: Feedback loop—after execution, verification gate runs; results and error signatures flow to xTrace and local memory for continuous improvement.
This pattern requires zero external packages (no pip install), uses our existing PowerShell and Python infrastructure, and composes naturally with OpenCode's task tool and skill system.
